expanded_description: Alcohols are organic compounds containing one or more hydroxyl ($-\text{OH}$) groups bonded to saturated carbon atoms. This section explores alcohol structure and classification, their distinctive physical properties arising from hydrogen bonding, systematic IUPAC nomenclature rules, and the relationship between molecular structure and reactivity. Alcohols are among the most important functional groups in organic chemistry, serving as solvents, intermediates, fuels, and components of biological molecules.

core_concept.principle: The Hydroxyl Group Determines Alcohol Properties and Reactivity
core_concept.explanation: The hydroxyl functional group ($-\text{OH}$) consists of an oxygen atom bonded to hydrogen and to a saturated carbon. Oxygen's high electronegativity ($3.5$ vs. carbon's $2.5$) creates a polar $\text{O}-\text{H}$ bond with partial negative charge on oxygen ($\text{O}^{\delta-}$) and partial positive charge on hydrogen ($\text{H}^{\delta+}$). This polarity enables hydrogen bonding between alcohol molecules (elevating boiling points) and with water (enhancing solubility). The $\text{O}-\text{H}$ bond can break heterolytically (making alcohols weakly acidic) or the $\text{C}-\text{O}$ bond can break (enabling substitution and elimination reactions).

image.alt: Diagram showing alcohol hydroxyl group with polar bonds and hydrogen bonding
image.caption: Alcohol hydroxyl group: polar $\text{O}-\text{H}$ bond ($\text{O}^{\delta-}-\text{H}^{\delta+}$) enables hydrogen bonding between molecules.

sections.introduction: Alcohols are organic compounds in which a hydroxyl group ($-\text{OH}$) is bonded to a saturated ($sp^3$ hybridized) carbon atom. The general formula for a simple acyclic alcohol is $\text{R}-\text{OH}$, where $\text{R}$ represents an alkyl group. Alcohols are ubiquitous in nature and industry: ethanol is the intoxicating component of alcoholic beverages and a common solvent; methanol is used as fuel and chemical feedstock; glycerol is a component of fats and oils; cholesterol and steroid hormones contain alcohol functional groups. Understanding alcohol structure and properties is essential for organic synthesis, biochemistry, and industrial applications.

key_concept.definition: An alcohol is an organic compound containing one or more hydroxyl ($-\text{OH}$) groups bonded to saturated ($sp^3$) carbon atoms. The general formula is $\text{R}-\text{OH}$ for monohydric alcohols, where $\text{R}$ = alkyl group.
key_concept.context: Alcohols are classified by the number of $-\text{OH}$ groups (monohydric, dihydric, polyhydric) and by the substitution pattern of the carbon bearing the $-\text{OH}$ group (primary, secondary, tertiary). This classification predicts physical properties and chemical reactivity.

image.alt: General structure of alcohols showing R-OH format
image.caption: Alcohol general structure: $\text{R}-\text{OH}$ where $\text{R}$ = alkyl group; oxygen is $sp^3$ hybridized with two lone pairs.

principles.principle: Hydroxyl Group Polarity and Hydrogen Bonding
principles.statement: The $\text{O}-\text{H}$ bond in alcohols is highly polar due to oxygen's electronegativity ($3.5$), creating partial charges $\text{O}^{\delta-}-\text{H}^{\delta+}$ that enable intermolecular hydrogen bonding.
principles.application: Hydrogen bonding between alcohol molecules significantly elevates boiling points compared to alkanes of similar molecular weight. Hydrogen bonding with water molecules makes low-molecular-weight alcohols miscible with water. For example, ethanol ($\text{MW} = 46$, $\text{BP} = 78^\circ\text{C}$) boils much higher than propane ($\text{MW} = 44$, $\text{BP} = -42^\circ\text{C}$).
principles.analogy: Think of hydrogen bonds as molecular 'handshakes': alcohol molecules can 'shake hands' with each other and with water molecules through their $\text{O}-\text{H}$ groups. Alkanes have no hands to shake (no polarity), so they don't stick together as strongly.
principles.example: Ethanol boiling point: $78^\circ\text{C}$ (H-bonding). Dimethyl ether ($\text{CH}_3\text{OCH}_3$, same molecular formula $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{O}$ but no $\text{O}-\text{H}$): $\text{BP} = -24^\circ\text{C}$. The $102^\circ\text{C}$ difference demonstrates hydrogen bonding's dramatic effect.

principles.statement: Alcohols are classified as primary ($1^\circ$), secondary ($2^\circ$), or tertiary ($3^\circ$) based on the number of carbon substituents attached to the carbon bearing the $-\text{OH}$ group.
principles.application: This classification predicts reactivity: primary alcohols oxidize to aldehydes then carboxylic acids; secondary alcohols oxidize to ketones; tertiary alcohols resist oxidation. Tertiary alcohols undergo $\text{S}_\text{N}1$ substitution and $\text{E}1$ elimination more readily than primary alcohols due to carbocation stability.
principles.analogy: Think of the $\text{C}-\text{OH}$ carbon as a person with arms: primary = one arm holding a carbon (two H's), secondary = two arms holding carbons (one H), tertiary = three arms holding carbons (no H). More carbon 'friends' stabilize positive charge during reactions.
principles.example: Primary: ethanol ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$), the $-\text{OH}$ carbon has one $\text{C}$ substituent. Secondary: isopropanol ($(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{CHOH}$), the $-\text{OH}$ carbon has two $\text{C}$ substituents. Tertiary: tert-butanol ($(\text{CH}_3)_3\text{COH}$), the $-\text{OH}$ carbon has three $\text{C}$ substituents.

components.definition: The alcohol functional group consists of an $sp^3$ hybridized oxygen atom bonded to hydrogen and to an $sp^3$ hybridized carbon. Oxygen has two lone pairs and forms two $\sigma$-bonds ($\text{C}-\text{O}$ and $\text{O}-\text{H}$) with bond angle $\approx 109^\circ$.
components.interpretation: The bent $\text{C}-\text{O}-\text{H}$ geometry (similar to water) and oxygen lone pairs create molecular polarity. The $\text{O}-\text{H}$ bond is the reactive site for acid-base chemistry; the $\text{C}-\text{O}$ bond is the reactive site for substitution/elimination.

image.alt: Diagram showing alcohol bond angles and lengths
image.caption: Alcohol structure: $sp^3$ oxygen with $\approx 109^\circ$ bond angle; polar $\text{O}-\text{H}$ and $\text{C}-\text{O}$ bonds.

sections.introduction: Alcohols can be classified in multiple ways: by the number of hydroxyl groups (monohydric, dihydric, polyhydric), by the substitution pattern of the hydroxyl-bearing carbon (primary, secondary, tertiary), and by the nature of the carbon skeleton (aliphatic, cyclic, aromatic). These classifications predict physical properties, spectroscopic signatures, and chemical reactivity patterns.

classifications.value: Classified as monohydric (one $-\text{OH}$), dihydric (two $-\text{OH}$), or polyhydric (multiple $-\text{OH}$)
characteristics: Dihydric (glycols): two $-\text{OH}$ groups, e.g., ethylene glycol
Polyhydric (polyols): three or more $-\text{OH}$ groups, e.g., glycerol
More $-\text{OH}$ groups = higher boiling point and water solubility
classifications.behavior: Each additional hydroxyl group increases hydrogen bonding capacity, elevating boiling point and water solubility. Polyhydric alcohols are often viscous liquids or solids at room temperature due to extensive intermolecular H-bonding.

examples.explanation: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$: one $-\text{OH}$ group. $\text{BP} = 78^\circ\text{C}$, miscible with water. Common solvent and beverage alcohol.

image.caption: Ethanol: monohydric alcohol with one hydroxyl group.

examples.explanation: $\text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$: two $-\text{OH}$ groups. $\text{BP} = 197^\circ\text{C}$, viscous liquid. Used as antifreeze due to low freezing point and high boiling point.
$\text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}(\text{OH})\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$: three $-\text{OH}$ groups. $\text{BP} = 290^\circ\text{C}$, very viscous. Component of triglycerides (fats and oils).

classifications.value: Classified by the number of carbon substituents on the hydroxyl-bearing carbon
characteristics: Primary ($1^\circ$): $-\text{OH}$ carbon bonded to one other carbon ($\text{RCH}_2\text{OH}$)
Secondary ($2^\circ$): $-\text{OH}$ carbon bonded to two other carbons ($\text{R}_2\text{CHOH}$)
Tertiary ($3^\circ$): $-\text{OH}$ carbon bonded to three other carbons ($\text{R}_3\text{COH}$)
Reactivity differs: oxidation and substitution patterns vary by class
classifications.behavior: Primary alcohols have two $\alpha$-hydrogens and oxidize to aldehydes then acids. Secondary alcohols have one $\alpha$-hydrogen and oxidize to ketones. Tertiary alcohols have no $\alpha$-hydrogens and resist oxidation. Carbocation stability: $3^\circ > 2^\circ > 1^\circ$ affects substitution/elimination rates.

examples.explanation: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$: $-\text{OH}$ carbon bonded to one carbon. Oxidizes to propanal then propanoic acid. Undergoes $\text{S}_\text{N}2$ substitution readily.

image.caption: Primary alcohol: $-\text{OH}$ carbon has one $\text{R}$ group and two $\text{H}$ atoms.

examples.explanation: $(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{CHOH}$: $-\text{OH}$ carbon bonded to two carbons. Oxidizes to acetone (ketone). Common disinfectant and solvent.
$(\text{CH}_3)_3\text{COH}$: $-\text{OH}$ carbon bonded to three carbons. Resists oxidation. Undergoes $\text{S}_\text{N}1$/$\text{E}1$ reactions via stable tertiary carbocation.

classifications.value: Classified by the nature of the hydrocarbon framework bearing the $-\text{OH}$ group
characteristics: Aliphatic alcohols: $-\text{OH}$ on open-chain or non-aromatic cyclic carbon
Cyclic alcohols: $-\text{OH}$ on cycloalkane ring (e.g., cyclohexanol)
Benzylic alcohols: $-\text{OH}$ on carbon adjacent to aromatic ring
Phenols: $-\text{OH}$ directly on aromatic ring (different class, more acidic)
classifications.behavior: Aliphatic alcohols have typical alcohol reactivity. Benzylic alcohols are more reactive due to resonance stabilization of intermediates. Phenols are distinctly different: more acidic ($\text{p}K_a \approx 10$ vs. $16-18$ for alcohols) due to aromatic resonance stabilization of phenoxide ion.

examples.explanation: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$: straight-chain alcohol. Typical alcohol properties and reactivity.

image.caption: Aliphatic alcohol: $-\text{OH}$ on open-chain saturated carbon.

examples.explanation: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{11}\text{OH}$: $-\text{OH}$ on cyclohexane ring. Secondary alcohol; oxidizes to cyclohexanone. $\text{BP} = 161^\circ\text{C}$.

$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$: $-\text{OH}$ on carbon adjacent to benzene ring. More reactive than typical primary alcohols due to benzylic stabilization. $\text{BP} = 205^\circ\text{C}$.

sections.key_concept: Alcohol classification: by $-\text{OH}$ count (mono/di/polyhydric), by carbon substitution ($1^\circ/2^\circ/3^\circ$), by skeleton (aliphatic/cyclic/benzylic). Classification predicts oxidation behavior and substitution/elimination reactivity.

sections.introduction: IUPAC nomenclature for alcohols follows systematic rules that clearly indicate the position of the hydroxyl group and the structure of the carbon skeleton. The suffix '-ol' replaces '-e' from the parent alkane name, and the chain is numbered to give the hydroxyl group the lowest possible position. Understanding these rules enables unambiguous communication of alcohol structures.

key_concept.definition: IUPAC alcohol names follow the format: [substituents]-[position]-[parent alkane]-ol. The parent chain must contain the $-\text{OH}$ group, and numbering prioritizes the hydroxyl position over substituents and multiple bonds.
key_concept.context: For simple alcohols, common names are still widely used (e.g., isopropyl alcohol, tert-butyl alcohol). However, systematic IUPAC names are required for complex molecules and ensure unambiguous identification. The $-\text{OH}$ group has high naming priority, second only to carboxylic acids and aldehydes.

image.alt: Diagram showing alcohol naming format with examples
image.caption: Alcohol naming: substituent positions + hydroxyl position + parent name with '-ol' suffix.

principles.statement: The parent chain must be the longest continuous carbon chain that contains the hydroxyl group, even if a longer chain without the $-\text{OH}$ exists.
principles.application: This rule ensures the functional group is always part of the parent name. A 5-carbon chain with $-\text{OH}$ takes priority over a 6-carbon chain without it. This is identical to alkene/alkyne nomenclature rules.
principles.analogy: Think of the $-\text{OH}$ group as the 'VIP' of the molecule: it must be included in the main party (parent chain) even if there's a bigger room (longer chain) available elsewhere. The functional group defines the molecule's identity.
principles.example: In a molecule with a 6-carbon chain (no $-\text{OH}$) and a 5-carbon chain (with $-\text{OH}$), the 5-carbon chain is the parent: pentanol, not hexane derivative.

principles.principle: Numbering Priority: Hydroxyl Group Over Substituents
principles.statement: The parent chain is numbered from the end that gives the hydroxyl group the lowest possible position number, even if this results in higher substituent numbers.
principles.application: Hydroxyl position takes priority over substituent positions and even over double/triple bond positions in some cases. Only when hydroxyl positions are equal from both ends do other features determine numbering direction.
principles.analogy: Like addressing a letter: the street name (hydroxyl group) is more important than the house number (substituent). Get the street right first, then optimize the house number.
principles.example: 4-methylpentan-2-ol (hydroxyl at 2) is correct, NOT 2-methylpentan-4-ol (hydroxyl at 4), even though 2 < 4 for the methyl group.

principles.statement: The suffix '-ol' replaces the '-e' from the parent alkane name, and the hydroxyl position is indicated by a number immediately before the suffix (e.g., pentan-2-ol) or before the parent name (e.g., 2-pentanol).
principles.application: Current IUPAC recommendations prefer pentan-2-ol format (position before suffix), but 2-pentanol is still widely accepted. For multiple hydroxyl groups, use '-diol', '-triol', etc., with all positions specified.
principles.analogy: The '-ol' suffix is like a flag marking 'hydroxyl group here!' The position number tells you exactly which carbon hosts this flag.
principles.example: Propan-1-ol ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$) vs. propan-2-ol ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}(\text{OH})\text{CH}_3$)—different compounds with different properties.

worked_examples.title: Naming a Simple Alcohol

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the longest chain containing the hydroxyl group.
Number from the end giving the hydroxyl group the lowest position.
Check for substituents: none present in this molecule.
Assemble the name with hydroxyl position.

worked_examples.final_answer: butan-2-ol. The hydroxyl group is at position 2, giving the lowest possible number. This is a secondary alcohol.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Hydroxyl position takes priority in numbering; suffix '-ol' indicates alcohol family.

worked_examples.title: Naming a Branched Alcohol

step_by_step.explanation: Find longest chain containing the hydroxyl group.
Number for lowest hydroxyl position.
Identify substituent position with chosen numbering.
Assemble name: substituent + hydroxyl position + parent.

worked_examples.final_answer: 4-methylpentan-2-ol. Hydroxyl position (2) takes priority over substituent position (4). This is a secondary alcohol.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Hydroxyl numbering priority over substituents; alphabetical ordering for multiple substituents.

image.caption: 4-Methylpentan-2-ol: 5-carbon parent with hydroxyl at C2 and methyl at C4.

worked_examples.title: Naming a Diol (Two Hydroxyl Groups)

step_by_step.explanation: Identify parent chain containing both hydroxyl groups.
Number from end giving lowest set of hydroxyl positions.
Use '-diol' suffix for two hydroxyl groups; specify both positions.
Assemble complete name.

worked_examples.final_answer: propan-1,3-diol. Both hydroxyl positions must be specified; '-diol' indicates two hydroxyl groups. Common name: trimethylene glycol.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Multiple hydroxyl groups use '-diol', '-triol' suffixes with all positions numbered.

sections.introduction: Alcohol physical properties are dominated by hydrogen bonding capability, which profoundly affects boiling point, melting point, viscosity, and water solubility. Understanding these property trends enables prediction of alcohol behavior in separation processes, solvent selection, and formulation chemistry.

categories.description: Alcohol boiling points are significantly elevated compared to alkanes of similar molecular weight due to intermolecular hydrogen bonding
categories.detailed_mechanism: Hydrogen bonds between alcohol molecules ($\text{R}-\text{O}^{\delta-}-\text{H}^{\delta+} \cdots \text{O}^{\delta-}-\text{R}$) require substantial energy to break during vaporization. Each additional $-\text{OH}$ group further elevates boiling point. Branching lowers boiling point by reducing surface area for intermolecular contact.

examples.explanation: Ethanol ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$, $\text{MW} = 46$): $\text{BP} = 78^\circ\text{C}$. Propane ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$, $\text{MW} = 44$): $\text{BP} = -42^\circ\text{C}$. The $120^\circ\text{C}$ difference demonstrates hydrogen bonding's dramatic effect.

image.alt: Graph comparing alcohol and alkane boiling points
image.caption: Alcohol boiling points are much higher than alkanes of similar molecular weight due to H-bonding.

examples.explanation: Isomeric butanols: 1-butanol ($1^\circ$, $\text{BP} = 118^\circ\text{C}$) > 2-butanol ($2^\circ$, $\text{BP} = 100^\circ\text{C}$) > tert-butanol ($3^\circ$, $\text{BP} = 83^\circ\text{C}$). More branching = lower BP due to reduced surface area.

Ethanol (one $-\text{OH}$, $\text{BP} = 78^\circ\text{C}$) < Ethylene glycol (two $-\text{OH}$, $\text{BP} = 197^\circ\text{C}$) < Glycerol (three $-\text{OH}$, $\text{BP} = 290^\circ\text{C}$). Each additional $-\text{OH}$ dramatically increases H-bonding.

categories.description: Low-molecular-weight alcohols are miscible with water; solubility decreases as hydrocarbon chain length increases
categories.detailed_mechanism: The polar $-\text{OH}$ group forms hydrogen bonds with water molecules, promoting solubility. However, the nonpolar hydrocarbon portion disrupts water's H-bond network. As chain length increases, the hydrophobic effect dominates. Rule of thumb: alcohols with $\leq 4$ carbons are miscible; $\geq 5$ carbons show limited solubility.

examples.explanation: Methanol ($\text{C}_1$): miscible. Ethanol ($\text{C}_2$): miscible. Propanol ($\text{C}_3$): miscible. Butanol ($\text{C}_4$): $8 \text{ g}/100 \text{ mL}$. Pentanol ($\text{C}_5$): $2 \text{ g}/100 \text{ mL}$. Hexanol ($\text{C}_6$): $0.6 \text{ g}/100 \text{ mL}$.

image.alt: Graph of alcohol water solubility vs. chain length
image.caption: Water solubility decreases as hydrocarbon chain length increases.

examples.explanation: Multiple $-\text{OH}$ groups increase water solubility even for larger molecules. Glycerol ($\text{C}_3$ with 3 $-\text{OH}$): miscible. Glucose ($\text{C}_6$ with 5 $-\text{OH}$): very soluble ($91 \text{ g}/100 \text{ mL}$).

categories.description: Alcohols are weak acids ($\text{p}K_a \approx 16-18$), weaker than water ($\text{p}K_a = 15.7$) but stronger than alkanes ($\text{p}K_a \approx 50$)
categories.detailed_mechanism: The $\text{O}-\text{H}$ bond can break heterolytically to release $\text{H}^+$, forming an alkoxide ion ($\text{R}-\text{O}^-$). Alkoxide stability determines acidity: electron-withdrawing groups increase acidity; electron-donating alkyl groups decrease acidity. Acidity order: methanol > primary > secondary > tertiary.

examples.explanation: Methanol ($\text{p}K_a = 15.5$) > Ethanol ($\text{p}K_a = 16$) > Isopropanol ($\text{p}K_a = 17$) > tert-Butanol ($\text{p}K_a = 18$). More alkyl groups = more electron donation = less stable alkoxide = weaker acid.

Alcohols react with sodium metal to form alkoxides and hydrogen gas: $2\text{R}-\text{OH} + 2\text{Na} \rightarrow 2\text{R}-\text{O}^-\text{Na}^+ + \text{H}_2\uparrow$. This reaction demonstrates alcohol acidity.

sections.key_concept: Alcohol physical properties: H-bonding elevates BP (much higher than alkanes); water solubility decreases with chain length ($\leq \text{C}_4$ miscible); weak acidity ($\text{p}K_a \approx 16-18$); acidity order: methanol > $1^\circ$ > $2^\circ$ > $3^\circ$.

sections.introduction: Several simple alcohols have major industrial, pharmaceutical, and biological importance. Understanding these common alcohols provides context for why alcohol chemistry is practically significant in everyday applications, from fuels and solvents to pharmaceuticals and biochemistry.

components.function: Industrial solvent; chemical feedstock (formaldehyde, acetic acid); fuel additive; antifreeze
components.key_feature: Simplest alcohol; highly toxic (causes blindness); $\text{BP} = 65^\circ\text{C}$; miscible with water; produced from synthesis gas ($\text{CO} + \text{H}_2$)

components.function: Beverage alcohol; disinfectant; solvent; fuel (bioethanol); chemical feedstock
components.key_feature: Most widely used alcohol; $\text{BP} = 78^\circ\text{C}$; miscible with water; produced by fermentation of sugars or ethylene hydration; denatured for industrial use

components.function: Disinfectant (rubbing alcohol); solvent; cleaning agent; de-icer
components.key_feature: Secondary alcohol; $\text{BP} = 83^\circ\text{C}$; miscible with water; evaporates quickly; effective against bacteria and viruses; less toxic than methanol
Dihydric alcohol; $\text{BP} = 197^\circ\text{C}$; viscous; sweet taste but toxic; lowers freezing point of water significantly

components.function: Food additive (humectant); pharmaceuticals; cosmetics; explosive production (nitroglycerin)
components.key_feature: Trihydric alcohol; $\text{BP} = 290^\circ\text{C}$; very viscous; sweet taste; non-toxic; byproduct of soap manufacturing (saponification)

components.function: Solvent; chemical intermediate; fuel additive; denaturant for ethanol
components.key_feature: Tertiary alcohol; $\text{BP} = 83^\circ\text{C}$; solid at room temperature ($\text{MP} = 25^\circ\text{C}$); resists oxidation; forms stable carbocation

sections.introduction: Advanced alcohol nomenclature involves handling multiple hydroxyl groups, substituents, unsaturation, and cyclic structures. This section provides systematic practice with increasingly complex structures to build naming proficiency for all alcohol types.

worked_examples.title: Naming an Unsaturated Alcohol

step_by_step.explanation: Find longest chain containing both the hydroxyl group and the double bond.
Number for lowest hydroxyl position (hydroxyl takes priority over double bond).
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Left-to-right: -OH at 4, C=C at 1. Right-to-left: -OH at 2, C=C at 4. Choose -OH at 2.}$

step_by_step.explanation: Assemble name: double bond position + parent + hydroxyl position + suffix.

worked_examples.final_answer: pent-4-en-2-ol. Hydroxyl position (2) takes priority in numbering; double bond position (4) is specified. The '-en-' infix indicates the double bond.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Hydroxyl group has higher naming priority than double bond; number for lowest -OH position.

image.caption: Pent-4-en-2-ol: 5-carbon chain with double bond at C4 and hydroxyl at C2.

worked_examples.title: Naming a Cyclic Alcohol
worked_examples.problem: Name: cyclohexane ring with $-\text{OH}$ at position 1 and methyl at position 3.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the ring as parent: cyclohexane with hydroxyl = cyclohexanol.
Number the ring starting at hydroxyl carbon (position 1), proceeding to give substituent lowest number.
Assemble name with substituent position.

worked_examples.final_answer: 3-methylcyclohexanol. The hydroxyl group automatically defines position 1 in cyclic alcohols; no need to specify '-1-ol'. Methyl is at position 3.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Cyclic alcohols: hydroxyl defines position 1 automatically; number ring to give substituents lowest positions.

image.caption: 3-Methylcyclohexanol: cyclohexane ring with -OH at C1 and methyl at C3.

worked_examples.title: From Name to Structure: 3-Ethylpentan-2-ol

step_by_step.explanation: Identify parent chain from base name: 'pentan-2-ol' = 5 carbons with hydroxyl at position 2.
Number the chain from left to right for drawing.
Add substituent at specified position: ethyl at C3.
Add hydrogens to satisfy tetravalency (each carbon needs 4 bonds).
Verify: count total carbons = 5 (parent) + 2 (ethyl) = 7 carbons.

worked_examples.final_answer: Condensed structure: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}(\text{OH})\text{CH}(\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3)\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3$. Molecular formula: $\text{C}_7\text{H}_{16}\text{O}$. This is a secondary alcohol.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Name-to-structure conversion: parent chain with hydroxyl first, then add substituents at specified positions, then saturate with hydrogens.

image.caption: 3-Ethylpentan-2-ol: pentane parent with hydroxyl at C2 and ethyl at C3.

worked_examples.title: Naming a Polyhydric Alcohol

step_by_step.explanation: Identify parent chain containing all hydroxyl groups.
Number from end giving lowest set of hydroxyl positions.
Use '-triol' suffix for three hydroxyl groups; specify all positions.
Assemble complete name.

worked_examples.final_answer: propan-1,2,3-triol. Common name: glycerol or glycerin. All three hydroxyl positions specified; '-triol' indicates three hydroxyl groups.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Polyhydric alcohols: '-diol', '-triol' suffixes with all hydroxyl positions numbered.

sections.section_title: Alcohol Nomenclature Summary and Common Mistakes
sections.introduction: A consolidated summary of alcohol naming rules helps prevent common errors. This section highlights critical rules, frequent mistakes, and provides a quick-reference checklist for naming any alcohol structure.

principles.statement: Alcohol nomenclature differs from alkane nomenclature in three key ways: suffix (-ol vs. -ane), parent chain selection (must contain -OH), and numbering priority (hydroxyl over substituents).
principles.application: When naming any compound with an -OH group, apply alcohol rules even if a longer alkane chain exists without the -OH. The functional group defines the molecule's identity.
principles.analogy: Alkane naming is like naming a street by its length. Alcohol naming is like naming a street by its landmark (the hydroxyl group)—the landmark takes priority over length.
principles.example: Alkane: 3-methylhexane (longest chain = 6). Alcohol: 4-methylhexan-2-ol (chain must contain -OH, numbered for hydroxyl position).

principles.statement: Current IUPAC recommendations place the hydroxyl position immediately before the '-ol' suffix (e.g., pentan-2-ol), though the older format (2-pentanol) remains acceptable.
principles.application: Use pentan-2-ol format for consistency with modern IUPAC standards. For multiple hydroxyl groups: propane-1,2-diol (all positions before suffix).
principles.analogy: Think of the position number as a label attached directly to the '-ol' flag: 'pentan-2-ol' means 'pentane with -ol flag at position 2'.

categories.description: Choosing the longest chain instead of the chain containing the hydroxyl group
categories.detailed_mechanism: Students often default to alkane rules and select the absolute longest carbon chain. For alcohols, the chain MUST contain the hydroxyl group even if shorter.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: 2-propylpentan-1-ol (7-carbon chain selected). CORRECT: 4-methylhexan-3-ol (6-carbon chain containing -OH). The hydroxyl must be in the parent.

categories.description: Numbering for lowest substituent position instead of lowest hydroxyl position
categories.detailed_mechanism: Alkane habit of minimizing substituent numbers conflicts with alcohol rule of minimizing hydroxyl numbers. Hydroxyl position always takes priority.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: 4-methylpentan-3-ol (numbered for methyl at 2). CORRECT: 2-methylpentan-3-ol (numbered for -OH at 3). Hydroxyl position 3 < 4, so this wins.

categories.description: Omitting the hydroxyl position number for alcohols with 3+ carbons
categories.detailed_mechanism: For methanol and ethanol, position numbers are unnecessary (only one possible position). For propanol and larger, the position MUST be specified to distinguish isomers.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: pentanol (ambiguous—which position?). CORRECT: pentan-1-ol or pentan-2-ol (specific position required). Propanol has two isomers; butanol has four isomers.

categories.description: Specifying position 1 for hydroxyl in cyclic alcohols or numbering incorrectly
categories.detailed_mechanism: In cyclic alcohols, the hydroxyl group automatically defines position 1. Writing 'cyclohexan-1-ol' is redundant. Number the ring to give substituents the lowest possible positions.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: 1-methylcyclohexan-1-ol (redundant -OH position). CORRECT: 2-methylcyclohexanol (-OH at 1 is implied; methyl at 2). The -OH position is automatically 1.

components.composition: Longest continuous chain containing the hydroxyl group
components.key_feature: Must contain -OH even if shorter chain exists without it

components.composition: Assign 1, 2, 3... from end nearest hydroxyl group
components.function: Gives hydroxyl the lowest possible position number
components.key_feature: Hydroxyl position priority over substituent positions

components.composition: Alkyl groups: methyl, ethyl, propyl, etc.
components.function: Identifies all branches off parent chain
components.key_feature: Use di-, tri-, tetra- for multiples; alphabetize substituents
Commas between numbers; hyphens between numbers and letters; no spaces
-OH automatically at position 1; number ring for lowest substituent positions

summary: Alcohols are organic compounds containing one or more hydroxyl ($-\text{OH}$) groups bonded to saturated ($sp^3$) carbon atoms, with general formula $\text{R}-\text{OH}$. The polar $\text{O}-\text{H}$ bond ($\text{O}^{\delta-}-\text{H}^{\delta+}$) enables hydrogen bonding, which dramatically elevates boiling points compared to alkanes and makes low-molecular-weight alcohols water-miscible. Alcohols are classified by: (1) number of $-\text{OH}$ groups (monohydric, dihydric, polyhydric), (2) carbon substitution ($1^\circ$, $2^\circ$, $3^\circ$), and (3) skeleton type (aliphatic, cyclic, benzylic). IUPAC nomenclature requires: (1) parent chain must contain the $-\text{OH}$ group, (2) numbering prioritizes lowest hydroxyl position over substituents, (3) suffix '-ol' replaces '-e' from parent alkane, (4) hydroxyl position specified for $\text{C}_3+$ alcohols (e.g., butan-2-ol). Multiple hydroxyl groups use '-diol', '-triol' suffixes with all positions numbered. Physical properties: boiling points increase with $-\text{OH}$ count and decrease with branching; water solubility decreases with chain length ($\leq \text{C}_4$ miscible); alcohols are weak acids ($\text{p}K_a \approx 16-18$) with acidity order: methanol > $1^\circ$ > $2^\circ$ > $3^\circ$. Common alcohols include methanol (toxic, feedstock), ethanol (beverages, fuel, solvent), isopropanol (disinfectant), ethylene glycol (antifreeze), and glycerol (food, cosmetics). Critical naming mistakes to avoid: selecting wrong parent chain (must contain $-\text{OH}$), wrong numbering direction (hydroxyl priority), missing position numbers, and redundant position specification in cyclic alcohols (hydroxyl = position 1 automatically).
